coredata.dc:title: Biomass-based carbon quantum dots for polycrystalline silicon solar cells with enhanced photovoltaic performance
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Xiaohui
Xu, Li
Ge, Shengbo
Foong, Shin Ying
Liew, Rock Keey
Fong Chong, William Woei
Verma, Meenakshi
Naushad, Mu.
Park, Young-Kwon
Lam, Su Shiung
Li, Qian
Huang, Runzhou
coredata.dc:description: Polycrystalline silicon solar cells modified using biomass resources are promising candidates to accomplish the goal of carbon neutrality. Developing a device with high power conversion efficiency (PCE) is important to resolve the ever-increasing energy shortage issues. Therefore, we develop a facile solution-casting approach to synthesise ethylene-vinyl acetate (EVA) films modified with ultra-high fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs). The films were coated on the surface of polycrystalline silicon solar cells and the PCE increased from 13.19% to 13.65%. The ultra-high fluorescent CQDs were prepared from Ginkgo biloba via a hydrothermal process, and different parts of Ginkgo biloba-based CQDs were investigated in various conditions. The ultra-high fluorescent CQDs obtained at 180 °C, 8 h and a reaction concentration of 0.048 g/mL from Ginkgo wood resulted in high fluorescence of up to 976.74 a.u.. At an excitation wavelength of 365 nm. This work substantially expands the potential of EVA films containing ultra-high fluorescent CQDs for applications in the photovoltaic, environment and energy fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ethylene-vinyl acetate
Carbon quantum dots
Solar cell
Ginkgo biloba
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CQDs from Ginkgo biloba
Effect of reaction temperature on the fluorescence of CQDs
Effects of reaction time on the fluorescence of CQDs
Effects of reaction concentration on the fluorescence of CQDs
Effect of different parts of Ginkgo biloba on the fluorescence of CQDs
Power conversion efficiency of polycrystalline silicon solar cells coated with CQDs/EVA films

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the financial support to Prof Lam by the Education University of Hong Kong to perform this project under International Grant (UMT/International Grant/2021/53456). The authors would also like to thank Saveetha Institute of Medical and Technical Sciences for the facilities and support provided to Prof Lam. The authors are grateful to the Researchers Supporting Project number (RSP2023R8), King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia for the financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the synthesis of ultra-high fluorescent Ginkgo biloba-based CQDs.

Figures and SEM images of different parts of Ginkgo biloba. (a) Picture of Ginkgo wood. (b) SEM image of Ginkgo wood. (c) Picture of Ginkgo bark. (d) SEM image of Ginkgo bark. (e) Picture of Ginkgo leaf. (f) SEM image of the front of Ginkgo leaf. (g) SEM image of the back of Ginkgo leaf. (h) The different CQDs solutions.

Photoluminescence intensity of Ginkgo biloba-based CQDs. (a) CQDs obtain from different temperatures: 160 °C, 180 °C, and 200 °C. (b) CQDs obtain at different times: 6 h; 8 h; 10 h. (c) CQDs obtain from different concentrations: 0.044 g/mL; 0.048 g/mL; 0.052 g/mL (d) CQDs obtain from Ginkgo bark, Ginkgo wood and Ginkgo leaf.

The characterisations of ultra-high fluorescent Ginkgo biloba-based CQDs. (a) TEM image of CQDs with lattice fringe. (b) The size distribution of CQDs. (c) UV–vis spectra of CQDs at 365 nm (Insert: CQDs solution under visible and 365 nm light). (d) XRD pattern of CQDs. (e) XPS spectrum of CQDs. (f) FTIR spectra of Ginkgo biloba wood powder and Ginkgo biloba wood-based CQDs.

The morphology of EVA and CQDs/EVA films containing different CQD amounts. (a–f) The SEM images of EVA and CQDs/EVA films. (g–l) The images of EVA and CQDs/EVA films under Confocal Laser Scanning Microscope. (m–r) The AFM phase images of EVA and CQDs/EVA films. (s–x) The AFM height images of EVA and CQDs/EVA films.

The characterisations of EVA and CQDs/EVA films. (a) The UV–vis transmittance spectra of EVA and CQDs/EVA films with different concentrations of CQDs. (b) The comparison of EVA and CQDs/EVA films under 365 nm ultraviolet and visible light. (c) The photoluminescence intensity of EVA and CQDs/EVA films. (d) The FT-TR spectra of EVA and CQDs/EVA films.

The photovoltaic performance of solar cell devices with EVA and CQDs/EVA films. (a) Schematic of Si-based solar cell stack. (b) The I–V curves of EVA and CQDs/EVA films. (c) The picture of CQDs/EVA film. (d) The EQE curves of devices with EVA and CQDs/EVA films.
Parameters involved in preparation of the ultra-high fluorescent Ginkgo biloba-based CQDs.
Photovoltaic parameters of polycrystalline silicon solar cells coating with EVA and CQDs/EVA films with different complex concentrations.

row.entry: EVA films with and without CQDs

head.ce:title: Biomass-based carbon quantum dots for polycrystalline silicon solar cells with enhanced photovoltaic performance

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Polycrystalline silicon solar cells modified using biomass resources are promising candidates to accomplish the goal of carbon neutrality. Developing a device with high power conversion efficiency (PCE) is important to resolve the ever-increasing energy shortage issues. Therefore, we develop a facile solution-casting approach to synthesise ethylene-vinyl acetate (EVA) films modified with ultra-high fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs). The films were coated on the surface of polycrystalline silicon solar cells and the PCE increased from 13.19% to 13.65%. The ultra-high fluorescent CQDs were prepared from Ginkgo biloba via a hydrothermal process, and different parts of Ginkgo biloba-based CQDs were investigated in various conditions. The ultra-high fluorescent CQDs obtained at 180 °C, 8 h and a reaction concentration of 0.048 g/mL from Ginkgo wood resulted in high fluorescence of up to 976.74 a.u.. At an excitation wavelength of 365 nm. This work substantially expands the potential of EVA films containing ultra-high fluorescent CQDs for applications in the photovoltaic, environment and energy fields.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: An increase in greenhouse gases has adversely affected global energy and raised environmental concerns. The China government has set an ambitious target to reach carbon neutrality before 2060. Using clean power has been proven to be an effective way to achieve this goal, including nuclear power, hydropower, wind, solar, and biomass [1,2]. Researchers have recently paid close attention to biomass and solar energy concerning their inexhaustibility, non-pollution and potential application. In general, solar energy has been widely used in conversion via photoelectric and photo-thermal. For instance, photovoltaic cells use semiconductors to convert light into electricity [3,4]. Notably, Si-type solar cells dominate most of the market demand owing to their excellent conversion performance, long life, and low cost [5]. Solar cells with single junctions can achieve approximately 33% power conversion efficiency (PCE) according to the Shockley-Queisser limit [6]. Nevertheless, the actual PCE of solar cells shows only 12−24% in laboratory or industrial production, which decelerates their further developments [7]. Hence, exploring new generation silicon-based solar cells with high PCE and environmentally benign is critical and urgent.

Great efforts have been devoted to manufacturing new-type silicon-based solar cells with efficient modules to achieve higher conversion efficiency. The spin coating of polycrystalline silicon solar cells with Indium Nitride particles (InN-Ps) has dramatically improved from 12.50 to 13.98% on PCE. However, the experimental process released toxic substances because the InN-Ps are fabricated via laser ablation from toxic chemicals, which hampers their potential applications [8]. Alternatively, adding promoters to ethylene-vinyl acetate (EVA) layers has been proven to be an effective way to improve PCE by encapsulating photovoltaic modules [9]. Consequently, the PCE improves from 12.15 to 12.29% by covering EVA films with modified nano-SiO2 and Eu3+ quaternary complexes [10]. Besides that, coating Eu3+ complex/EVA films onto lanthanide complexes could increase the PCE of solar cells from 15.06 to 15.57% [11]. However, these lanthanide complexes have limitations such as scarcity, high cost, poor photostability and thermostability. Biomass-based materials can be an alternative to lanthanide complexes due to their high fluorescence, sustainable resources, facile synthesis methods, low cost, and pollution-free strategy [12].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is profoundly effective in fluorescent materials and have evoked tremendous attention during the past two decades due to their extraordinary intrinsic performances and promising applications [13]. CQDs have been widely applied in bioimaging, metal-based fluorescent probes, and photocatalysis due to their high photoluminescence, photoinduced electron transfer, biocompatibility, nontoxicity, and eco-friendly features [14–17]. Recently, CQDs have been utilised in solar cells. The composite films fabricated from CQDs obtained from citric acid, thiourea and vinyl acetate-ethylene enhanced the PCE from 17.44 to 17.86% [18]. However, the contaminants released from the preparation process of CQDs are a longstanding unsettled issue. Pollution-free CQDs can be produced from biomass material and green synthesis methods. For instance, orange waste peel-based CQDs [19], banana juice-based CQDs [20] and fast-growing poplar-based CQDs [21] fabricated via hydrothermal carbonisation produced contaminant-free and fluorescence CQDs. Nevertheless, the current CQDs encounter relatively low fluorescence, which severely hinders the high fluorescence of CQDs. Consequently, exploring a new generation of biomass-based CQDs with high fluorescence via a green and facile strategy is highly desirable but still remains a formidable challenge. Although substantial studies have explored solar energy and biomass energy, almost no study combines these two energies to create novel materials and to make better use of this energy [22].

Herein, we provide an effective strategy to enhance the PCE of polycrystalline silicon solar cells by coating EVA films containing ultra-high fluorescent CQDs. The ultra-high fluorescent CQDs are prepared from biomass-based materials (Ginkgo biloba) via hydrothermal carbonisation. Additionally, the influences of various preparation temperatures, times and reaction concentrations on the fluorescence of Ginkgo wood-based CQDs, Ginkgo bark-based CQDs and Ginkgo leaves-based CQDs are investigated. Eventually, we doped the ultra-high fluorescent CQDs into the EVA matrix by solution-casting method to produce CQDs/EVA films, which improves photoelectric efficiency remarkably. The new CQDs/EVA films could be an ultra-high fluorescent and reproducible film material for applications in photovoltaic, energy sciences, and environmental fields.

The CQDs were produced from Ginkgo biloba (including Ginkgo wood, Ginkgo bark and Ginkgo leaf) from the campus of Nanjing Forestry University. Ginkgo biloba was cleaned with deionized water and dried in an oven to ensure that its surface was free of impurities. EVA copolymer (28% VA) and deionized water were purchased from Chengying Precision Instrument Co., LTD. (Jiangsu, China). Toluene was purchased from Nanjing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All reagents were used as received.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs from Ginkgo biloba
ce:section.ce:para: Ginkgo biloba powder was obtained by smashing Ginkgo biloba with a pulveriser and sieved with a 100-mesh standard sieve. The 100-mesh Ginkgo powder was dried in an electric thermostatic drying oven at 103 °C for several hours until completely dry. The powder was then sealed with plastic wrap and stored in a hermetic bag for later use.

The CQDs/EVA films with different proportions were prepared by the solution-casting method using ultra-high fluorescent Ginkgo wood-based CQDs powder. The powder-like EVA (2.0 g) was dissolved in toluene (25 mL) at room temperature and stirred for 10 min (Fig. S1a). Different amounts of CQDs powder (0–25 mg) were put into the mixture (EVA and toluene) and was dispersed with ultrasonic dispersion for 1 h (Fig. S1b). During heating, the toluene evaporated, and EVA solidified slowly, wrapping with CQDs powder. EVA contains VA, which is a silane coupling agent. Coupling agent acted as a bridge to connect the carbon point with EVA. The whole process contains physical adhesion and chemical binding. In a petri dish (100 mm), the mixture was poured and dried for 30 min at 75 °C, the product after dried was fluorescent polymer composite films [24,25]. Moreover, a thin film on the surface of polycrystalline silicon solar cells (rated voltage: 2.0 V; active area: 5.0 cm2) was fabricated directly to test their photovoltaic performance further. All films with a different weight ratio of CQDs powder were obtained using the same method described above.

The JEOL, JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (TEM) with ultra-high resolution and a Quanta 200 scanning electron microscope (SEM) manufactured by FEI company were used to acquire the images of fluorescent CQDs and CQDs/EVA films. The UV absorbance spectra were measured with a UV spectrophotometer (UV–vis, Lambda 950, PerkinElmer company). The UV result was measured from CQDs solution. After obtaining CQDs solution, CQDs solution was diluted with deionized water to ensure qualitative analysis. The UV results were measured from diluted CQDs solution using deionized water solution as the reference sample. The photoluminescence (PL) intensity was examined using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (LS 55, PE company). The Fourier Transformed Infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra were obtained from a spectrophotometer (Vertex 80 V, Bruker, German). X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopic (XPS) results were obtained from X-ray Photoelectron Spectrometer (AXIS Ultra-DLD). X-ray Diffraction (XRD) measurement was obtained from an X-ray diffractometer (Ultima Ⅳ, Rigaku). 2θ angle is scanned from 10° to 80° at a scan rate of 10°/min. For CQDs/EVA, a LSM701 confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) was used to examine the CQDs distribution within the EVA matrix. The surface morphology was detected by Atomic Force Microscope (AFM, Dimension Edge). The I–V characteristics of the films were tested by the solar simulator (91, 192 STS, Newport Corporation) at AM 1.5G standard conditions. External quantum efficiency (EQE) measurement was recorded using a QEX10 PV system. A Keithley 2400 source-measurement unit measured the I–V curves of polycrystalline silicon solar cell devices under AM 1.5G spectra from a solar simulator. National Renewable Energy Laboratory (NREL) monocrystal silicon reference cells were used to calibrate the illumination intensity of the solar simulator. After coating the photovoltaic module with a CQDs/EVA film, measurements were taken. Each sample was measured four times for accurate data. External quantum efficiency (EQE) was further performed to investigate the photon response of polycrystalline silicon solar cells at a different wavelength and confirm the reliability of the photocurrents from the I–V measurements.

The ultra-high fluorescent CQDs selected for the further experiment depended on the influences of reaction time, temperature, and reaction concentration towards the PL intensity measured by the fluorescence spectrophotometer of the CQDs via hydrothermal process. The optimal conditions for preparing ultra-high fluorescent CQDs from the following pristine materials were investigated. The morphologies of different parts of Ginkgo biloba were studied by SEM. Their microstructure could intuitively present the structure of pristine materials and process of transforming Ginkgo biloba into CQDs. Fig. 2 (a, c and e) shows the pictures of Ginkgo biloba as follows: Ginkgo wood, Ginkgo bark and Ginkgo leaf. The surface of Ginkgo wood is uniform and flat, with many uneven burrs remaining on the surface (Fig. 2b). The obtained Ginkgo wood-based CQDs have better fluorescent properties than other wood-derived CQDs, like pine wood-based CQDs [26], poplar wood-based CQDs [27], and balsa wood-based CQDs [28]. The inherent constituents in the tissues of Ginkgo wood might be worth further exploration. The TEM images of Ginkgo biloba wood depict the microstructure of Ginkgo wood tissues, indicating that converting pristine materials to CQDs probably appears at the edge of the tissues as shown in Fig. S2 (a and b).

Besides that, irregular pores were observed in Ginkgo bark. The distribution and dimension of pores are haphazard and non-uniform (Fig. 2d). This microstructure might provide a complete carbonisation condition for preparing fluorescent CQDs, which has not been reported until now [29]. As shown in Fig. S2 (c and d), the microcosmic tissue of Ginkgo bark has more accumulation of dark matter. Fig. 2 (f and g) presented the two sides of Ginkgo leaf tissue, distribution uniformly, where the tiny bulges and dents arrange along the direction of the leaf stem. Fig. S2 (e and f) displays the microstructure tissues of Ginkgo leaves.

Interestingly, the fluorescence of Ginkgo leaves-based CQDs differs from other leaves-derived CQDs, such as bamboo leaves [30], lotus leaves, and pine needles [31]. When excited at 365 nm, pine needles-based CQDs present a strong fluorescence up to 610 a.u., which is far superior to Ginkgo leaves-based CQDs. We are aware of no previous instances of similar study producing CQDs from Ginkgo wood and bark to compare the fluorescence from three parts of Ginkgo biloba. Our work expands the potential for usage of Ginkgo biloba and provides the possibility for industrial production of CQDs.

To gain the optimal conditions for synthesising ultra-high fluorescent CQDs, we carry out different experimental groups in various preparation conditions according to Table 1. From left to right are Ginkgo wood-based CQDs, Ginkgo bark-based CQDs and Ginkgo leaves-based CQDs, respectively (Fig. 2h). Fig. S3a exhibits the 8-h heating performed on mother liquor of Ginkgo wood-based CQDs solution (0.048 g/mL) at 180 °C. With the process of high-speed centrifugation and dialysis, the CQDs solution is obtained as shown in Fig. S3b. We also take the SEM and cold field emission-SEM (FE-SEM) images of CQDs solution for further observation (Figs. S4a and b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of reaction temperature on the fluorescence of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Ginkgo wood-based CQDs exhibit a shift in maximum PL intensity peak from 425 to 492 nm as the excitation wavelength increases from 340 to 390 nm, illustrating that CQDs can downshift the high-energy UV light to low-energy visible light. Additionally, the CQDs present ultra-high fluorescence (976.74 a.u.) when the excitation wavelength is 365 nm. Therefore, the fluorescence spectrophotometer with excitation wavelength at 365 nm is performed (Fig. S5a). Fig. 3 a depicts the photoluminescence intensity of Ginkgo wood-based CQDs synthesised via the carbonisation method at different temperatures for 8 h and a reaction concentration of 0.048 g/mL (Fig. S5b). As the reaction temperature increases, the photoluminescence intensity curves of Ginkgo wood-based CQDs present significant changes. At 160 °C, the PL intensity of CQDs is weak. When the temperature reaches 180 °C, the PL intensity reaches its maximum value at 976.74 a.u.. However, at 200 °C, the PL intensity is far weaker than others. It was speculated that the fluorescence intensity of CQD produced at the temperature of 160 °C is lower due to the incomplete carbonisation of wood powder, which would lead to low quantum yields simultaneously and thus limit their fluorescence intensity [32]. The CQD prepared at 200 °C exhibits a low fluorescent intensity, which might be ascribed to the excessive carbonisation of wood powder, resulting in an unfavourable optical performance. These experimental results are consistent with those reported in the previous literature [33]. Therefore, 180 °C is the optimal reaction temperature for synthesising ultra-high fluorescent CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of reaction time on the fluorescence of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3b presents the effects of different reaction times on the PL intensity of Ginkgo wood-based CQDs at 180 °C and a reaction concentration of 0.048 g/mL. Several data with different times varying from 4 to 12 h were selected to explain the effects of reaction time on the synthesis of fluorescent CQDs (Fig. S5c). With the prolonging of reaction time, the obtained CQDs show a pronounced variation trend. It shows that the PL intensity of 8 h-CQDs was slightly higher than 6 h-CQDs but decreased sharply for 10 h-CQDs. We predicted that a reaction time of 6 h resulted in incomplete carbonisation processes [34]. The reaction time of 10 h implies that the long carbonisation time leads to excessive carbonisation that affects the fluorescent intensity [35]. Thus, the reaction time of 8 h is the best condition to produce ultra-high fluorescent CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of reaction concentration on the fluorescence of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3c exhibits the PL intensity of Ginkgo wood-based CQDs with different concentrations at 180 °C and a reaction time of 8 h. We prepare ultra-high fluorescent CQDs with diverse concentrations from 0.040 g/mL to 0.060 g/mL (Fig. S5d). The increase in reaction concentrations from 0.044 g/mL to 0.048 g/mL increases the PL intensity, ultimately reaching its peak with a reaction concentration of 0.048 g/mL. Nevertheless, when the reaction concentration increases from 0.048 g/mL to 0.052 g/mL, the PL intensity declines, suggesting that both low and high concentrations could weaken the PL intensity [36]. The reaction concentration impacts the PL intensity of CQDs, as the attraction and aggregation of CQDs under high concentration lead to aggregation-caused-quenching (ACQ) phenomenon to further reduce PL intensity [37]. The interaction of fluorescent substances may result in energy transfer and thus reduce the fluorescence performance. Hence, a reaction concentration of 0.048 g/mL is ideal for preparing ultra-high fluorescent CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of different parts of Ginkgo biloba on the fluorescence of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Given that the optimal preparation conditions have been obtained, CQD's feedstock needs to be optimised further to obtain ultra-high fluorescent CQDs accurately. Ginkgo bark-based CQDs and Ginkgo leaves-based CQDs fabricate according to the conditions above to compare with Ginkgo wood-based CQDs. Fig. 3d presents the PL intensity of CQDs prepared from Ginkgo bark, Ginkgo wood and Ginkgo leaves. The PL intensity of Ginkgo wood-based CQDs is remarkably higher than Ginkgo bark-based CQDs and Ginkgo leaves-based CQDs. We conjecture that Ginkgo bark has a porous structure, which could lead to an excessive carbonisation state to impede its PL intensity. The PL intensity of Ginkgo leaves-based CQDs is different from previous reports [38], showing a low fluorescent emission peak. The results are dedicated to different states of leaves that Ginkgo leaves-based CQDs (wilted or fresh leaves) [39]. Overall, the ultra-high fluorescent CQDs from the optimised parameters shows high fluorescence of up to 976.74 a.u. at 365 nm (excitation wavelength).

We characterise and analyse the ultra-high fluorescent CQDs to further investigate their inherent merits. Fig. 4 a shows the TEM images of CQDs. We could detect that quasi-spherical CQDs are well dispersed with no aggregation. The lattice fringes with a d-spacing of 0.27 nm correspond to the graphitisation structure of CQDs with a narrow observation field (red arrows). The size distribution was from 0.4 nm to 2.5 nm, and the average particle diameter was 1.12 nm, calculated from 100 random particles (Fig. 4b). As presented in Fig. 4c, due to complex electron transition on the surface, CQDs present two peaks at 276 nm and 340 nm, which derive from π-π* transition of C=C bands and n-π* transition of C–O bands, respectively. The insert photographs clarify CQD solutions under visible and UV light, respectively. Under visible light, they appear yellowish. However, when exposed to UV light at 365 nm, they glow in a blue-green colour, which suggests their potential applications in bioimaging and anti-counterfeiting fields. From the XRD pattern, we discovered the characteristic peak of CQDs diffraction at about 23.5°, these may be representative of diffraction planes of sp2 graphitic carbon that exactly correspond to the d-spacing of 0.27 nm seen in TEM images (Fig. 4d).

The chemical analysis of the CQDs were explored via XPS, and three peaks were found at 285.2 eV, 399.3 eV and 533.3 eV, which relates to C 1s (59.06%), O 1s (39.68%), and N 1s (1.26%), respectively (Fig. 4e). In order to elucidate the chemical band types of CQDs, we tested the C 1s spectrum and the O 1s spectrum. The C1s spectrum could be divided into three curves, which were C–C/C=C/C–H (284.8 eV), C–O (286.3 eV), and CO (288.1 eV) (Fig. S6a). The O 1s spectrum presents three characteristic peaks at 531.9 eV, 533.5 eV and 535.1 eV, which were assigned to the C=O bond, C–O–C bond, and C–OH bond, respectively (Fig. S6b). The high content of C and O demonstrates that CQDs compose of carbonic materials and abundant oxygen-containing functional groups, which is in accordance with the findings reported by Mehta [38]. The FT-IR spectra compared Ginkgo wood-based CQDs and Ginkgo wood powder (Fig. 4f). The absorption peak at 3407 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of the O–H bond, while those at 2930 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of the C–H band, which is in line with existing work [40]. The CQDs also appear in two strong peaks at 1630 cm−1 and 1420 cm−1, assigned to the stretching vibration of C=C bonds and C–H plane deformation vibration on the aromatic nucleus, respectively. A vibrational band at 1741 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibration of C=O of carbonyl of pristine material. The peak at 1512 cm−1 appeared due to the existence of an aromatic skeleton. The characteristic absorption peak of in-plane deformation vibration of the aromatic C–H bond appears at 1054 cm−1. The results validate that the structure of Ginkgo wood powder is partially destroyed in hydrothermal carbonisation. There are some hydroxyl groups, methyl groups, carboxyl groups, and abundant oxygen-containing bonds on the surface of CQDs, which guarantees the stability of CQD and is further used in subsequent practical applications [41].

Different concentrations of CQDs powder were used to prepare the transparent CQDs/EVA films using the solution-casting method. Fig. 5 (a-f) shows the surface microstructure of films, demonstrating that the EVA films have remarkably smooth and uniform surfaces. When CQD powder is introduced to EVA films, the surface morphology becomes rough but uniform, demonstrating that a little amount of CQD powder can disperse uniformly in the EVA film matrix. As the amount of CQDs powder increases, a spot-like surface of CQDs/EVA films forms, implying that exagerrated CQD powder can unite to produce uneven protuberances as a result of mixing with larger particles [42]. We also find that the distribution of protuberances becomes disordered evidently with increasing CQDs powder. During the solution-casting process, the excessive CQD particles become tighter as the solvent evaporates, demonstrating that increased CQD doping concentration leads to poor compatibility between the CQD powder and EVA matrix. However, the surface of CQDs/EVA films shows significant protuberances due to the effect of CQDs powder dispersion and aggregation degrees in EVA films. It also demonstrates that the addition of CQD powder can significantly change the surface morphology of CQDs/EVA films.

An excitation wavelength of 365 nm was applied to examine the CLSM images to uncover the CQDs powder distribution within the EVA matrix, conspicuous blue fluorescence was observed as seen in Fig. 5 (g-l). No fluorescence is observed from EVA film without CQDs. It shows that the blue fluorescence is homogeneously distributed with no aggregation when the additional amount of CQD powder is below 10 mg. However, CQD powder gathers into larger particles after the additional amount of more than 10 mg of CQD powder. The aggregations are probably caused by the CQD particles absorbed and enwrapped the neighbouring CQD particles [43]. With the increase of CQDs powder amount, the blue fluorescence becomes stronger, affirming that bright PL emission of Ginkgo wood-based CQDs sources within their intrinsic carbon core of band gap and external surface defect. The phase and height images of the atomic force microscope for the surface morphology of EVA and CQDs/EVA films are shown in Fig. 5 (m-x). The EVA and EVA films containing CQD powder (0.025 wt% and 0.050 wt%) are dense and uniform, manifesting that a little CQD powder could disperse homogeneously in the EVA matrix.

Nevertheless, it appears substantial protuberances and bubbles when the additional amount of CQDs exceeds 10 mg. The distribution of protuberances with different shapes and sizes is extremely disordered, which illustrates that excessive CQD powder unites into particles gradually when the doping concentration of CQDs increases to a certain degree, resulting in the inhomogeneity of the CQDs/EVA films surfaces. It further illustrates that excessive CQD particles have poor compatibility in the EVA matrix. It is therefore expected that fluorescent CQDs/EVA film is suitable for developing environment-friendly and efficient photovoltaic devices, demonstrating a promising prospect for implementing the CQDs/EVA films in the photovoltaic field [44].

The absorption spectra were shown to identify the intrinsic property of the EVA and CQDs/EVA films (Fig. 6 a). The transmittance spectra obtained by measuring the absorbance at 365 nm revealed a broad range within 225–275 nm, which corresponded to the UV absorption of the CQDs powder and nearly covered the transition of π–π* as shown in the absorption spectra. Under visible light, the light transmittance of the CQDs powder (0.025 wt%) film was better than others. However, transmittance of fluorescent films drastically declines with the increasing doping concentration of CQDs. This result is consistent with the large grain size in the CLSM images, resulting in a strong absorption in the UV–vis spectra. As plotted in Fig. 6b, the transmittance and transparency of CQDs/EVA films decrease sharply under visible light and 365 nm ultraviolet light, indicating that the high concentration of CQDs powder in the EVA matrix leads to fluorescence quenching. Excited state reaction, resonant energy transfer, non-fluorescent complex, molecular collision, pH change, temperature change and pressure change may cause fluorescence quenching. The fluorescence of CQDs is well performed while they are uniformly dispersed in solution. As the CQDs solution concentration increases, the excess CQDs powder will gather together to form agglomerations, further leading to the weakening or even disappearance of the fluorescence of CQDs. It is caused by the π - π bond of CQDs or exciplex consuming the excited state energy. It also suggests that the excessive CQD particles agglomerate in the EVA matrix. Thus, the transmittance and transparency of CQDs/EVA films decrease compared to the control, which indicates that the addition of CQDs powder plays a key role in decreasing the transmittance and transparency of CQDs/EVA films. This result is in accordance with the SEM images. As shown in Fig. 6c, the PL properties of the EVA films with or without CQDs powder are studied, revealing an apparent peak at approximately 362–370 nm. Intriguingly, the PL intensity of EVA films with or without CQDs powder alters marginally along with the increase of CQDs powder amount. The CQDs/EVA films show a similar variation trend, illustrating that the CQDs powder amount of EVA films on the PL intensity is negligible [45]. Nevertheless, the PL intensity of CQDs/EVA films changes significantly compared to EVA film, which may be attributed to excessive aggregation of CQDs powder in the EVA matrix. It further suggests that increasing the CQDs powder amount leads to poor compatibility between CQDs powder and EVA matrix.

The FT-IR spectra are conducted to acquaint further with the influence of incorporating CQD powder on forming CQDs/EVA films (Fig. 6d). The variation trends of EVA films with or without CQDs were similar, suggesting that only natural physical adhesion occurs between CQD powder and EVA matrix. When EVA begins to solidify, it will cover CQD powder naturally. The peak at 3440 cm−1 was ascribed to the stretching vibration of O–H bonds in acetic acid. This result is in line with the previous study [46]. The C=O stretching leads to a vibration bond at 1734 cm−1 whereas the C–O stretching observed at 1238 cm−1.

Additionally, a tiny peak at 2362 cm−1 was similar to the FT-IR spectrum of CQDs, indicating that CQDs powder exists in the EVA matrix. When EVA film was incorporated with CQD powder, they bond to each other by hydrogen bonds, which facilitates the crystallinity of CQDs and raises the stability of CQDs/EVA films. The EVA films with or without CQDs powder are full of carbon and oxygen-containing functional groups, which are environment-friendly materials and easily degrade. Considering the excellent performance of CQDs/EVA films, they are recognised to be the most appropriate choice for solar cell applications, environment and energy fields.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Power conversion efficiency of polycrystalline silicon solar cells coated with CQDs/EVA films
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the photovoltaic performance of solar cells devices with EVA films with or without CQDs, we measured the I–V curves of polycrystalline silicon solar cells coated with EVA and CQDs/EVA films, which includes the main parameters of open-circuit voltage (V OC), short-circuit current density (J SC), fill factor (FF) and PCE (Table 2 ). The schematic of the Si-based solar cell stack is presented in Fig. 7 a. We have developed a general method for coating CQDs/EVA films on Si-based solar cells to maximise PCE performance. The photovoltaic module was coated with a CQDs/EVA film prior to measurements as demonstrated in Fig. 7b. The integrated J SC extracted from EQE values were consistent with the current densities from the I–V measurement. Beyond that, we also measure the PCE and EQE of devices without EVA and CQDs/EVA films for further comparison (Fig. S7). We observed the appreciable performance of Si-type solar cells topped with CQDs/EVA film when the doping concentration of CQDs powder equals 0.050 wt%, the PCE was improved up to 13.65% (Fig. 7c). The slightly enhanced PCE could attribute to the introduction of CQDs powder, which leads to light absorption in the photoactive layer and the increase of FF from 72.11 to 72.86% [47]. It can be elucidated that CQD powder is well-distributed in the EVA matrix, therefore leading to the highly efficient photovoltaic performance of 13.65%. However, the PCE decreases slightly when the doping concentration of CQDs raises to a certain degree (after exceeding 0.050 wt%), illustrating that excessive CQDs seriously hinder the transmittance and transparency of films. Thus, the resulting materials block their absorption in the visible wavelength range and impede their performance. It also suggests that excessive CQDs could quench their fluorescence and lead to severe carrier recombination by aggregation [48].

As shown in Fig. 7d, EQE curves were measured at wavelengths between 300 and 1100 nm to examine how CQDs/EVA films affect photovoltaic response characteristics of Si-based solar cells. A notable increase of EQE value appeared in the wavelength range of 300–600 nm, which was attributed to the photons undergoing down-shift through the CQDs powder incorporated within the EVA matrix [49]. Although the curves were similar, the EQE value for the 0.050 wt% CQDs/EVA film-based device was slightly higher than others, indicating the enhanced photoresponse within the polycrystalline silicon solar cells. The excessive CQDs forming agglomerations diminish the transparency and weaken their photon absorption of films in the visible region and further damage carrier transportation and photoresponse, leading to a decrease in device performance.

In this work, we report an effective strategy to enhance the PCE of the polycrystalline silicon solar cells from 13.19% to 13.65% by coating 0.050 wt% CQDs/EVA films. The ultra-high fluorescent CQDs were obtained from Ginkgo wood via hydrothermal synthesis. The different proportions of CQDs/EVA films were prepared by a facile solution-casting method. Although the films were coated on the solar cell surface, the devices still exhibited enhanced results compared to the controlled devices. It was revealed that the polycrystalline silicon solar cells encapsulated with CQDs/EVA films improved the PCE of devices. The fluorescent CQDs/EVA films exhibit great potential for photovoltaic applications in the environmental and energy fields.

Xiaohui Wang: Conceptualization, Writing - Original Draft. Li Xu: Writing - Original Draft, Visualisation. Shengbo Ge: Conceptualization, Writing - Original Draft. Shin Ying Foong: Writing - Review & Editing. Rock Keey Liew: Writing - Review & Editing. William Woei Fong Chong: Writing - Review & Editing. Meenakshi Verma: Writing - Review & Editing. Mu.Naushad: Writing - Review & Editing, Funding acquisition. Young-Kwon Park: Writing - Review & Editing. Su Shiung Lam: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Qian Li: Writing - Review & Editing. Runzhou Huang: Supervision, Conceptualization, Writing - Original Draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support to Prof Lam by the Education University of Hong Kong to perform this project under International Grant (UMT/International Grant/2021/53456). The authors would also like to thank Saveetha Institute of Medical and Technical Sciences for the facilities and support provided to Prof Lam. The authors are grateful to the Researchers Supporting Project number (RSP2023R8), King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia for the financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

TEM images of Ginkgo wood, Ginkgo bark and Ginkgo leaves; PL intensity of CQDs at various excitation wavelength; PL intensity of Ginkgo biloba based CQDs at various temperatures, times and concentrations; the picture of CQDs in different states; SEM images and FE-SEM images of Ginkgo biloba based CQDs solution; the fitting curves of CQDs in XPS pattern; the photoelectric efficiency of bare cell.
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.energy.2023.127354.
